title: 狄更斯在布鲁克林

note:
甚至在大流行之前，整个时尚业就已经开始分崩离析了。既然没有人有理由打扮起来，现在会发生什么呢？作者：Irina Aleksander照片：Stephanie Gonot·2020年8月6日。
要准确地确定美国对冠状病毒的恐慌是什么时候发生的，但3月12日是突出的。商店的罐头食品都卖完了。街道上空无一人。汤姆·汉克斯的病毒检测刚刚呈阳性。那天晚上，时装设计师斯科特·斯特恩伯格(Scott Sternberg)醒着躺在洛杉矶银湖(Silver Lake)社区的家中，想着他两年前创立的基础系列Entireworld。人们还会买衣服吗？他到底有多少现金才能坚持下去呢？他什么时候要裁员呢？他说：“我的外来者战斗伤疤刚刚大开。”
“局外人乐队”是斯特恩伯格以前的公司。他于2004年创立了该公司，最初是一系列修身衬衫和领带。(还记得紧身领带热潮吗？这是斯特恩伯格说的。)。最终，它成长为完整的男装和女装系列，以自觉的预科生服装赢得了时尚界的青睐。斯特恩伯格将美国时装设计师协会(C.F.D.A)的两个奖项带回家。颁奖典礼，相当于业界的奥斯卡颁奖典礼。他摆好姿势与坎耶·韦斯特合影。米歇尔·奥巴马(Michelle Obama)穿着他的一件连衣裙。他在东京和纽约开了店。然后，在2015年，出乎所有人的意料，斯特恩伯格宣布Band即将倒闭。与一些比利时人的一项投资已经失败，但这感觉并不是事情的全部。斯特恩伯格知道整件事的来龙去脉。他在Entireworld做出的每一个选择都是为了防止这种情况再次发生。现在，一场全球大流行来袭了。他无法预见到这一点。没有人这么做。
与其他设计师不同，斯特恩伯格学习的不是设计，而是经济学，他之所以选择这个专业，部分原因是他进入圣路易斯华盛顿大学(Washington University)的那年，那里的教授、经济学家道格拉斯·诺斯(Douglas North)获得了诺贝尔奖。斯特恩伯格以优异成绩毕业。他的毕业论文是关于好莱坞演员经济学的，这就是他最初来到洛杉矶的原因。这一切都是说，斯特恩伯格知道不确定性对消费者行为的影响。
他说：“我脑子里想的是：天哪，我不知道大企业将如何处理这件事。”“但对于一家小企业来说，这足以让我们所有人都出局”--他打了个响指--“一枪就够了。”
碰巧的是，首先倒下的是巨人。在接下来的几个月里，J.Crew、Neiman Marcus、Brooks Brothers和J.C.Penney申请破产。GAP公司。无法支付其2785家北美门店的租金。到了7月份，黛安·冯·芙丝汀宝(Diane Von Fursten Berg)宣布将裁员300人，并关闭19家门店中的18家。对小企业的迫在眉睫的损害是不可想象的。
第二天早上，也就是星期五，斯特恩伯格驱车前往Entireworld在韩国城的办公室。他在办公桌前坐下，开始起草一封电子邮件：“哇。我是说，去他妈的。“。
他没有让他的工作人员运行这封电子邮件。没有关于这件事的会议。他只是坐下来写了下来。
“我已经病了吗？我能离开家吗？我该怎么跟我的员工说？我妈妈今天坐飞机回家会没事吗？佐德“--斯特恩伯格的狗--会感染冠状病毒吗？我买的T.P.够了吗？这会持续多久？谁是负责人？下一步是什么？“。
3月15日周日，这封电子邮件发给了该品牌的3万名订户。在一片每天的促销电子邮件的海洋中，这是一封明显的人类电子邮件。但这仍然是一次促销：对于运动衫来说，这是该品牌最畅销的产品，在行业中是“英雄物品”。Entireworld的运动服灵感来自一部法国儿童电影，采用了欢快的颜色棱镜，在斯特恩伯格的愿景中，“让你看起来像是‘皇家特南鲍姆’(The Royal Tenenbaum)中的天线宝宝本·斯蒂勒(Ben Stiller)和1979年J.C.彭尼(J.C.Penney)广告的混合体。”
没过多久，斯特恩伯格的员工就开始给他发笑脸表情。该品牌仍处于初创阶段，平均每天售出46件运动衫。那一天，他们卖出了1000多台。当运动服用完后，购物者会在T恤、袜子和内衣中走动。截至月底，该品牌的销售额比去年3月增长了662%。
我们相遇的那一天，4月24日，是该公司历史上收入最高的一天。那天早上来了一批新货，很快又销售一空。Entireworld现在在两个月内的总收入超过了其开业第一年的全年收入。
说到“见面”，我的意思是我们在斯特恩伯格的后院，坐在相距20英尺的椅子上，中间放着消毒湿巾。在这一点上，斯特恩伯格并没有太多地离开家，而是依靠餐包服务BlueApron的送货维持生计，并将配料定量分配到多顿饭中。Entireworld的常务董事乔丹·希夫(Jordan Schiff)-前多夫·查尼(Dov Charney)的美国服装公司(American Apparel)的前身，斯特恩伯格(Sternberg)的全盛时期公开向其致敬-刚刚与新冠肺炎一起下台。但他仍在追踪这些数字。就在几天前，希夫报告说，该公司的600双薰衣草女式袜子已经销售一空。
斯特恩伯格心情很好。这显然不仅仅是因为一封电子邮件。这也不仅仅是因为美国在可预见的未来已经适应了运动裤。自从“局外人乐队”倒闭后，他就一直在做这些基础工作。Entireworld不仅仅是名义上的偏离，它实际上表明了与In人群相反的情况。这也是斯特恩伯格对传统时尚体系的拒绝，这一体系曾使他一举取得成功。不再有时装秀，不再有季节性系列，不再有不可靠的批发账户(R.I.P.Barney)，也不再需要支付所有费用的加价。(Band的衬衫起价220美元；Entireworld的起价95美元。)。
多年来，斯特恩伯格一直在说，时尚业是一个正在走向崩溃的巨大泡沫。现在，大流行只是加速了不可避免的情况。事实上，它已经开始了。目前仓库和商店里堆满了令人难以置信的过剩服装，其中一些可能永远不会重新开张。“整个频道都死了，”斯特恩伯格说。“而且没有迹象表明它什么时候会再次开机。”
4月份，美国的服装销售额下降了79%，这是有记录以来最大的降幅。然而，运动裤的购买量上升了80%。Entireworld就像是在世界末日中幸存下来的罕见的生命形式。通过押注奢侈品市场将会失败，斯特恩伯格避开了拖累该行业其他公司的力量。“因为你可以看到不祥之兆，”他说。“尼曼人在墙上写字，巴尼人。..。听着，巴尼斯？这对任何人来说都不是什么令人震惊的事情。“
我会记得时尚业的最后几天，因为它已经存在了20年，这是马克·雅各布斯(Marc Jacobs)从纽约美世酒店(Mercer Hotel)现场直播，戴着珍珠项链，化着完美的妆。转播在两个不同的虚拟事件上进行，时长为75分钟。它始于4月15日，从“Vogue”的“全球对话”(Global Conversations)开始，这是该杂志推出的一个系列节目，目的是想办法修复时尚业。一个月后的5月15日，该杂志继续在该行业的首选新闻网站“时尚商业”(Business Of Fashion)上展开讨论。
雅各布斯告诉“Vogue”杂志：“我现在正处于悲痛之中。”
“我不确定会不会有春夏‘21’系列。”
雅各布斯是来看他2020年秋季秀的，作为一种告别。“我已经对我的心理医生，我可爱的理查森医生说了这句话，”他在从他的VAPE笔中吸了很长一口气后告诉“时尚商业”杂志，“如果这是我最后一场时装秀，我会非常高兴的。”那个收藏品永远不会被制作出来。买家不能下订单，即使他们下了订单，工厂也会关闭。雅各布斯说，他不得不解雇“一群人”，并要求其他人接受减薪。这并不是说这是从大流行开始的。自2013年以来，雅各布斯的业务已经从250家门店缩减到只有4家。在接受“Vogue”采访时，他说，“我认为，很长一段时间以来，这是一个非常困难的行业。”
2005年的情况看起来不同了。我选择那一年有点主观，因为那是我开始在“女装日报”实习的时候。在美国时尚界，这是一段激动人心的时刻。一批新的年轻设计师刚刚进入舞台，取代了20世纪80年代和90年代的明星(唐娜·卡兰(Donna Karan)、卡尔文·克莱因(Calvin Klein)、迈克尔·科尔斯(Michael Kors)等)。让跑道重新充满活力。实习生看到的东西不多，但时装周的邀请函偶尔也会冒出来。我的第一场演出是扎克·波森(Zac Posen)，大概在第8排，第二场是普罗恩扎·舒勒(Proenza Schouler)。那些设计师，连同王大仁(Alexander Wang)、德里克·林(Derek Lam)、菲利普·林(Phillip Lim)、Rag&amp；bone、罗达特(Rodarte)、贾森·吴(Jason Wu)，以及后来的约瑟夫·奥图扎拉(Joseph Altuzarra)，似乎在商店买家和时尚编辑的帮助下一夜之间成长为全球品牌，他们急于引入后9/11一代的美国人才。
局外人乐队就是其中的一部分。2004年，斯特恩伯格29岁时创立了这个品牌。罗达特姐妹没有接受过正规培训，和父母一起住在加利福尼亚州帕萨迪纳。斯特恩伯格曾是创意艺术家经纪公司(Creative Artists Agency)的经纪人，在当时的时尚沙漠设计一条生产线。和她们一样，斯特恩伯格很快就受到了局外人的欢迎。几个月后，他在GQ有了一整页的领带照片，并被巴尼斯接走了。“我们紧挨着德里斯、巴黎世家、普拉达，”他说。“而‘我们’是...。我在洛杉矶做衬衫和领带。“。
与桑姆·布朗(Thom Browne)等品牌一样，班德也加入了书呆子般的预科生复兴的浪潮-缩小的上衣、马球衫、船鞋-或者说斯特恩伯格所说的“关于预科生服装的预科生服装”。一旦他进军女装领域，该品牌就成长为1500万美元的批发业务，在全球250家门店销售。Sternberg说：“显然，到最后对我们来说并不是那么好，因为我们没有建立一个健全的业务。”“但是，这个全球时尚体系所能做的事情的力量是相当令人难以置信的。”
当斯特恩伯格说“全球时尚体系”时，他指的是设计师、时尚媒体和商店的生态系统，它让我们所有人都穿上了衣服。时装周是这些实体聚会的地方。春季系列在秋季展出的原因是(反之亦然)，这样它们就可以在实际的季节里被及时订购、审查和生产。就像大多数事情一样，这个系统被互联网颠覆了。一旦普通人可以在网上查看收藏品-令人困惑的是，他们直到六个月后才能购买-一切都开始加速。现在商店需要提前送货来满足需求，两次送货根本不够。突然之间，中季系列-主要是秋前系列和度假村系列(也被称为邮轮)-成为了常态，即使是对规模较小的设计师来说也是如此，他们的客户不一定是飞往卡普里或圣特罗佩斯过冬的一小部分人。
因此，设计师们从一年制作两个系列变成了四个系列。如果你有一个男士系列，也许实际上是6个，如果你是迪奥或纪梵希，你也在做高级定制。由于蕾哈娜(Rihanna)或安妮·海瑟薇(Anne Hathaway)或坐在前排的任何人，时装秀已经成长为大型营销活动，这些系列中的每一个也都是一场秀。不知何故，这一切仍然进行得相当顺利。消费者在消费，商店买家买得更多，设计师生产得更多、更快。生意蒸蒸日上。每个人都在不断成长。
如果有转折点的话，那可能是2008年秋天。那一年，纽约时装周吸引了大约23.2万人参加，创造了4.66亿美元的游客支出。在9月份结束三天后，经济崩溃了。奢侈品市场已经过饱和，现在已经没有人买了。商店惊慌失措，所有东西都提早降价。但第二年和后年，他们又做了一次，依靠降价来创造收入，并培训消费者进行打折购物。所以现在你的夏季连衣裙在一月份到货，甚至在天气允许你穿之前就打折了。
时尚循环不再有意义。尽管预算不断减少，但每两个月仍有数千人飞往世界各地观看演出。设计师们开始跟不上节奏，最引人注目的是约翰·加利亚诺(John Galliano)，他将2011年的反犹咆哮(随后被迪奥解雇)归因于与工作相关的压力。衣服本身也变得有点怪异。提速的日历催生了“无季节着装”，这是弗兰肯斯坦服装的一种潮流：无趾靴子，无袖外套-你懂的。当你在7月份交付秋季产品时，真的不再是天气的问题了。
这可能是重新考虑事情的时候了。相反，每个人都加倍努力，做了更多的东西。
随着像Net-a-Porter和Matches Fashion这样的在线零售商获得了吸引力，所有的东西突然变得无处不在，百货商店寻找新的方式来吸引顾客。进入“新奇”，这是一个术语，有时是字面上的钟声和口哨，买家越来越多地要求设计师添加到系列中，以吸引像猫一样迷路的顾客。如果在过去的十年里，你去寻找一件简单的羊绒毛衣，却遇到了一件拉链、巨大的动物脸、闪闪发光的肩膀或任何“痛苦”的东西-那就是新奇的。如果你发现自己被激怒了，你并不孤单。“这是为了我们可以卖给萨克斯、内曼、巴尼、诺德斯特龙、科莱特，每个人都可以有自己特别的东西，”斯特恩伯格回忆道。“我基本上是在做我不喜欢的东西，因为我认为买家想要，甚至顾客也不想要。”
过去，商店以专属设计师的承诺来吸引购物者。一旦设计师再也负担不起独家专卖店的费用，妥协的办法就是独家款式。除了展示的系列之外，买家们还要求稍微修改一下外观-这里加长下摆，那里增加袖子，把裙子上的印花做成裤子-然后可能是他们的顾客独家拥有的。这件事还在继续。巴特谢瓦·海伊(Batsheva Hay)曾是一名诉讼律师，2016年开始了她的同名草原连衣裙系列。她告诉我：“你为独家品牌做的工作量已经失控了。”“‘我想要这个，你能用这个做一点吗？’...‘。有一部分是因为他们认为它可能会卖出去，但也有一部分只是因为他们可以说它是独家的。“。
莫莉·纳特(Molly Nutter)曾是巴尼斯百货公司负责销售的副总裁，她在百货公司工作了19年。“这个体系已经被打破了很长一段时间，”纳特说，他现在是德克萨斯州奥斯汀一家专卖店by George的总裁。“设计师们面临很大的压力，要求他们生产更多的系列，从而生产更多的产品。我要说的是，这并不是顾客的真正需求；我认为这只是零售商试图抢占市场份额。他们认为，如果我能更早地进入更多的市场，那么我就可以吸引更多的客户。但由于每个人都在这么做，这只会让问题变得更加复杂。当然，所有这些商店最终都有太多的库存，所有的促销活动都是从这里开始的。你基本上是在把奢侈品放在那里，几乎立刻让它贬值。这就是这个恶性循环。“。
这就是雅各布斯后来在他的酒店房间里哀悼的东西。当每个人似乎都急于定义时尚的未来时，他却在为它的现在留出空间。他头脑清醒，坦率，或多或少比任何人都聪明。(当他拒绝为这篇文章接受采访时，我松了一口气。)
雅各布斯告诉“Vogue”杂志：“我们做的每件事都做得太过分了，以至于没有消费者购买。”“每个人都被它弄得筋疲力尽。设计师们被它弄得筋疲力尽。记者们追随它已经筋疲力尽了。“。他补充说，“当你只是被要求拿出作品时，就像是拿枪指着你的头，然后说，你知道，跳舞，猴子！”
当时巴尼斯的首席执行官马克·李(Mark Lee)倒台了，斯特恩伯格说，他过度承诺了百货商店能够卖出多少库存。斯特恩伯格说：“巴尼斯向我们许诺全世界，但从来没有兑现过任何承诺。”(李明博没有回复记者的置评请求。)。“我们听他们的话是愚蠢的。但我们信任他们。那是个彻头彻尾的杀手。你觉得没有安全感，就像，我需要巴尼斯冷静一下。然后是这些被称为R.T.V.的东西。“。
R.T.V.是“退货给卖家”的缩写，听起来是这样的：如果一个系列--商店要求你用新奇和独家商品填充的系列--卖不出去，零售商可以退货并要求退款。根据Nutter的说法，由于商店举步维艰，这笔交易的条款变得更糟。在某些情况下，商店要求设计师以寄售方式销售，或者如果某个百分比的系列没有以全价出售，则分担成本。因此，假设一家商店决定提早减价：你现在欠它这些损失。“就在我告诉你这件事的时候，”纳特说，“我想，这不是很疯狂吗？”
它是。这太疯狂了。这就是事情变得更加疯狂的地方：为了保护排他性，商店不得不承诺进行更大规模的购买，订购的衣服超过了他们可能售出的数量。然后，当他们搬不动东西的时候，他们就会退还。多亏了快时尚的兴起和奢侈品市场同时试图跟上它不可能的步伐，所有这些都开始感觉是一次性的。生产过剩和奢侈品打折的循环是如此有害，以至于英国品牌博柏利(Burberry)在2018年透露，为了保持“品牌价值”，该公司每年焚烧价值3700万美元的商品--不是比喻的，而是字面上的：焚烧。
简而言之，时尚似乎正在慢慢消亡。还记得时装周吗？在遭受所有这些损失的同时，设计师们仍然大约每三个月举办一次时装秀，制作费用高达数十万美元。(如果你是香奈儿，那就是数百万。)。问题是，每个参加节目并通过Instagram上没完没了的模糊视频流传出去的人都在积极地为自己的工作辩护。因为如果你在那里通过手机上的小屏幕观看，而真正的现场表演正在几英尺远的地方进行，为什么还要去呢？“上帝保佑时尚媒体，”斯特恩伯格说。“他们还没有意识到每个人都在同时看到它。”
“这是一个小小的骗局，时装周，”他继续说。“我喜欢做节目，但你会沉迷其中。然后你就停不下来了。因为如果你停下来，他们会写你停下来，你会看起来像个失败者。否则商店就不再买你的东西了，你不知道他们为什么要买你的东西，但他们会买的。如果你不是在做节目，你就不再重要了。“。
斯特恩伯格承认，还有其他因素扼杀了外来者乐队，其中最主要的是他自己在扩展利基品牌方面缺乏经验，但最终他资金不足，杠杆率过高。他在苏豪区开店的那天-附上了一根Momofuku牛奶棒-他知道一切都结束了。斯特恩伯格从CLCC获得了200万美元的可转换贷款，CLCC是一家由比利时航运大亨支持的时尚基金，六个月后违约。品牌是抵押品。(自那以后，乐队重生为僵尸版的自己，由比利时人运营。)。2015年5月，他在加州波莫纳交出了密码、钥匙和一个储物柜，以及该品牌的档案，然后离开了。“但这不是什么大灾难，”他说。“嗯……。到了最后，这有点像是一场灾难。“。
斯特恩伯格的故事在他的同龄人中并不是独一无二的。在欧洲，路威酩轩(LVMH)和开云集团(Kering)等奢侈时尚集团将年轻设计师与经验丰富的商人配对。“在美国，创业精神要强得多，”理论创始人之一、普罗恩扎和Rag&amp；bone的早期投资者安德鲁·罗森(Andrew Rosen)告诉我。“你有很多才华横溢的年轻设计师，坦率地说，他们没有商业合作伙伴来配合。”
我问斯特恩伯格，他是否觉得自己失去了叙述。“在某种程度上，我没有丢失n。
.
note_en:
Even before the pandemic, the whole fashion industry  had started to unravel. What happens now that no one  has a reason to dress up?  By Irina Aleksander  Photographs by Stephanie Gonot • August 6, 2020
 to pinpoint when exactly panic about coronavirus took hold in the United States, but March 12 stands out. Stores ran out of canned goods. Streets emptied of cars. Tom Hanks had just tested positive for the virus. That evening, Scott Sternberg, a fashion designer, was lying awake at home in the Silver Lake neighborhood of Los Angeles, thinking about Entireworld, a line of basics he founded two years earlier. Would people still buy clothes? How much cash did he have to keep going? When would he have to lay people off? “My Band of Outsiders battle scars just opened wide,” he said.
 Band of Outsiders was Sternberg’s previous company. He founded it in 2004 as a line of slim shirts and ties. (Remember the skinny-tie boom? That was Sternberg.) Eventually it grew into full men’s and women’s collections that won over the fashion world with self-consciously preppy clothes. Sternberg took home two Council of Fashion Designers of America (C.F.D.A.) awards, the industry’s equivalent of the Oscars. He posed for photos with Kanye West.  Michelle Obama wore one of his dresses. He opened stores in Tokyo and New York. Then, in 2015, to everyone’s surprise, Sternberg announced that Band was going out of business. An investment with some Belgians had gone bad, but that didn’t feel like the whole story. Sternberg knew the whole story. Every choice he made at Entireworld was to prevent it from happening again. Now a global pandemic had hit. He couldn’t foresee that. No one did.
 Unlike other designers, Sternberg studied not design but economics, a major he chose in part because the year he entered Washington University in St. Louis, the economist Douglass North, a professor there, won a Nobel Prize. Sternberg graduated summa cum laude. His senior thesis was about the economics of actors in Hollywood, which is how he wound up in Los Angeles in the first place. This is all to say that Sternberg knew what uncertainty does to consumer behavior.
  “What was going through my head was: Man, I don’t know how big businesses are going to deal with this,” he said. “But for a small business this is enough to take all of us out” — he snapped his fingers — “in one shot.”
 As it happened, it was the giants who would fall first. Over the next few months, J. Crew, Neiman Marcus, Brooks Brothers and J.C. Penney filed for bankruptcy. Gap Inc. couldn’t pay rent on its 2,785 North American stores. By July,  Diane von Furstenberg announced she would lay off 300 employees and close 18 of her 19 stores. The impending damage to small businesses was inconceivable.
 The next morning, a Friday, Sternberg drove to Entireworld’s offices in Koreatown. He sat down at his desk and began drafting an email: “Wow. I mean, WTF.”
 He didn’t run the email by his staff. There was no meeting about it. He just sat down and wrote it.
 “Am I sick already? Can I leave my house? What do I tell my employees? Will my mom be OK on her flight home today? Can Zod” — Sternberg’s dog — “get coronavirus? Did I buy enough T.P.? How long will this last? Who’s in charge? What’s next?”
 The email went out to the brand’s 30,000 subscribers on Sunday, March 15. It was, in a sea of daily promotional emails, a distinctly human one. But this was still a promotion: for a sweatsuit, the brand’s top seller, a “hero item” in industry speak. Inspired by a French children’s film, Entireworld’s sweatsuits come in a prism of cheery colors and, in Sternberg’s vision, “sort of make you look like a cross between a  Teletubbie, Ben Stiller in ‘The Royal Tenenbaums,’ and a J.C. Penney ad from 1979.”
 It wasn’t long before Sternberg’s employees began texting him happy-face emoji. On an average day, the brand — still in its nascent stage — sells 46 sweats. That day they sold more than 1,000. When they ran out of sweatsuits, shoppers moved through the T-shirts, socks and underwear. By month’s end, the brand’s sales were up 662 percent over March the previous year.
 The day we met, April 24, was the highest-grossing day in the company’s history. A new shipment came in that morning and promptly sold out again. Entireworld had now grossed more in two months than in its entire first year in business.
 By “met,” I mean that we were in Sternberg’s backyard in chairs positioned 20 feet apart, with a setup of disinfectant wipes between us. At this point, Sternberg hadn’t been leaving the house much, instead subsisting on deliveries from BlueApron, the meal-kit service, and rationing the ingredients into multiple meals. Entireworld’s managing director, Jordan Schiff — formerly of  Dov Charney’s American Apparel, whose heyday Sternberg’s line openly pays homage to — had just come down with Covid-19. But he was still tracking the numbers. Just a few days before, Schiff reported that the company had sold out of  600 pairs of lavender women’s socks.
 Sternberg was in a good mood. This was obviously not just because of an email. Nor was it simply because America had settled into sweatpants for the foreseeable future. He’d been laying down this groundwork since Band of Outsiders imploded. Entireworld wasn’t a departure in name only, suggesting as it does the opposite of the in crowd. It was also Sternberg’s rejection of the traditional fashion system, the one that once vaulted him to success. No more fashion shows, no more seasonal collections, no more wholesale accounts that had become unreliable (R.I.P. Barneys) or the markups required to pay for it all. (Band’s shirts started at $220; Entireworld’s are $95.)
  For years, Sternberg had been saying that the fashion industry was a giant bubble heading toward collapse. Now the pandemic was just speeding up the inevitable. In fact, it had already begun. An incredible surplus of clothing was presently sitting in warehouses and in stores, some of which might never reopen. “That whole channel is dead,” Sternberg said. “And there’s no sign of when it’s turning on again.”
  In April, clothing sales fell 79 percent in the United States, the largest dive on record. Purchases of sweatpants, though, were up 80 percent. Entireworld was like the rare life form that survives the apocalypse. By betting that the luxury market would fail, Sternberg had evaded the very forces that were bringing down the rest of the industry. “Because you could see the writing on the wall,” he said. “The Neimans writing on the wall, the Barneys. ... Listen, Barneys? That was not a shock to anyone.”
 that I will remember from the last days of the fashion industry as it has existed for the last two decades, it’s Marc Jacobs streaming live from the Mercer Hotel in New York   in pearls and perfect makeup. The broadcast ran to 75 minutes in length over two different virtual events. It began on April 15, with Vogue’s Global Conversations, a series the magazine introduced to figure out how to fix the fashion industry, and continued a month later, on May 15, with Business of Fashion, the industry’s go-to news website.
 “I’m in the process of grief right now,” Jacobs told Vogue.
    “I’m not sure there will be a spring/summer ’21 collection.”
  Jacobs had come to see his fall 2020 show as a kind of farewell. “I’ve said this to my psychiatrist, my lovely Dr. Richardson,” he told Business of Fashion, after taking a long drag from his vape pen, “that I would be very happy if that were my last show.” That collection would never be produced. Buyers couldn’t place orders, and even if they had, factories were shut down. Jacobs said he had to lay off “a bunch of people” and ask others to take pay cuts. Not that this began with the pandemic. Since 2013, Jacobs’s business had shrunk from 250 stores to just four. Speaking to Vogue, he said, “This has been a very difficult business to be in for a long time, I think.”
 Things looked different in 2005. I’m choosing that year somewhat subjectively, because that’s when I started as an intern at  Women’s Wear Daily. It was a thrilling time in American fashion. A new guard of young designers had just entered the scene, displacing the stars of the 1980s and ’90s (Donna Karan, Calvin Klein, Michael Kors, et al.) and re-energizing the runways. Interns don’t see much, but occasionally fashion week invites trickle down. My first show was Zac Posen, in something like Row 8. My second was Proenza Schouler. Those designers, along with  Alexander Wang, Derek Lam, Phillip Lim, Rag & Bone, Rodarte, Jason Wu and later Joseph Altuzarra, seemed to grow into global brands overnight, with the help of store buyers and fashion editors eager to usher in a post 9/11 generation of American talent.
 Band of Outsiders was part of that. Sternberg was 29 when he started the brand in 2004. Like the   Rodarte sisters, who had no formal training and lived with their parents in Pasadena, Calif., Sternberg, a former agent at Creative Artists Agency designing a line in what was then a fashion desert, was an outsider instantly embraced. Within months he had a full-page photo of his ties in GQ and was picked up by Barneys. “We were next to  Dries, Balenciaga, Prada,” he said. “And ‘we’ were ... me, making shirts and ties in L.A.”
 Along with brands like  Thom Browne, Band joined the wave of the nerdy-preppy resurgence — shrunken blazers, polos, boat shoes — or what Sternberg called “preppy clothes about preppy clothes.” Once he expanded into women’s wear, the brand grew into a $15 million wholesale business, sold in 250 stores worldwide. “It wasn’t by the end all that good for us, obviously, because we weren’t building a sound business,” Sternberg said. “But it’s pretty incredible the power of what that global fashion system could do.”
 When Sternberg says “global fashion system,” he’s referring to the ecosystem of designers, fashion media and stores that puts us all in clothes. Fashion week is where those entities meet. The reason spring collections are shown in the fall (and vice versa) is so they can be ordered, reviewed and produced in time for the actual season. As with most things, this system was upended by the internet. Once normal people could view collections online — which, confusingly, they couldn’t buy until six months later — everything began to accelerate. Now stores needed deliveries earlier to fill demand, and two deliveries simply weren’t enough. Suddenly midseason collections — mainly, pre-fall and resort (also known as cruise) — became the norm, even for smaller designers whose customers were not necessarily among the small subset of people who jet off to Capri or St.-Tropez for the winter months.
 So designers went from making two collections a year to four. If you had a men’s line, maybe it was actually six, and if you were Dior or Givenchy, you were also doing couture. As fashion shows had grown into huge marketing events because  Rihanna or Anne Hathaway or whoever was sitting in the front row, each of those collections was also a show. Somehow this was all still going pretty well. Consumers were consuming, store buyers were buying more and designers produced more and faster. Business boomed. And everyone just kept growing.
 If there was a turning point, it might have been fall 2008. That year, New York Fashion Week drew an estimated 232,000 attendees and generated $466 million in visitor spending. Three days after it ended in September, the economy collapsed. The luxury market was already oversaturated, and now there was no one to buy the stuff. Stores panicked and marked everything down early. But then they did it again the next year, and the year after that, relying on markdowns to generate revenue and training consumers to shop on sale. So now you had summer dresses arriving in January and being discounted before the weather would even allow you to wear them.
 The fashion cycle stopped making sense. Despite dwindling budgets, thousands of people were still flying all over the world every two months for the shows. Designers started to crack under the pace, most notably John Galliano, who attributed his  2011 anti-Semitic rant (and subsequent firing from Dior) to work-related stress. And the clothes themselves got kind of weird. The sped-up calendar gave birth to “seasonless dressing,” a trend of Frankenstein clothing items:  toeless boots, sleeveless coats — you get it. When you’re delivering fall in July, it’s really not about the weather anymore.
 This might have been the time to rethink things. Instead, everyone doubled down and made more stuff.
 As online retailers like Net-a-Porter and Matches Fashion gained traction, and everything was suddenly sold everywhere, department stores looked for new ways to draw customers. Enter “novelty,” a term for the sometimes-literal bells and whistles that buyers increasingly asked designers to add to collections in order to entice straying customers like cats. If in the last decade you’ve gone looking for a simple cashmere sweater and instead encountered ones with zippers, giant animal faces, glitter shoulders or “distressed” anything — that’s novelty. If you found yourself annoyed, you were not alone. “That was so we could sell to Saks, Neiman, Barneys, Nordstrom, Colette, and everybody could have their own special thing,” Sternberg recalled. “I was basically making stuff I didn’t like because I thought a buyer wanted it, not even the customer.”
  It used to be that stores attracted shoppers with the promise of an exclusively carried designer. Once designers could no longer afford to remain exclusive to a certain store, the compromise was exclusive styles. In addition to a presented collection, buyers requested slightly altered looks — lengthen a hem here, add a sleeve there, take the print from that dress and make it into pants — that could then be exclusive to their customers. This is still going on. “The amount of work you do for exclusives is out of control,”  Batsheva Hay, a former litigator who started her namesake line of  off-kilter prairie dresses in 2016, told me. “ ‘I want  this, can you make  this with a little  this. ... ’ Some of it is because they think it might sell, but some is just so they can say it’s exclusive.”
 Molly Nutter, a former V.P. for merchandising at Barneys, worked for the department store for 19 years. “The system has been broken for a long time,” said Nutter, who is now the president of ByGeorge, a specialty store in Austin, Texas. “There was a lot of pressure on designers to produce more collections, and therefore more product. I would say it wasn’t a real demand by the customer; I think it was just retailers trying to grab market share. They thought, If I can get more in, and earlier, then I can get more clients through my door. But with everyone doing this, it just compounds the problem. Then of course all of these stores end up with too much inventory, and this is where all of the promotional activity starts to take place. You’re basically putting luxury product out there and devaluing it almost right away. It was just this vicious cycle.”
 This is what Jacobs would later be mourning in his hotel room. While everyone seemed eager to define fashion’s future, he was holding space for its present. He was lucid, candid, somehow smarter than everyone. (I was relieved when he declined to be interviewed for this article.)
 “We’ve done everything to such excess that there is no consumer for all of it,” Jacobs told Vogue. “Everyone is exhausted by it. The designers are exhausted by it. The journalists are exhausted from following it.” He added, “When you’re just told to produce, to produce, to produce, it’s like having a gun to your head and saying, you know,  Dance, monkey!”
  down with the chief executive at Barneys at the time, Mark Lee, who Sternberg says overpromised how much inventory the department store would be able to sell. “Barneys promised us the world and never delivered on any of it,” Sternberg said. (Lee did not respond to requests for comment.) “And it was stupid of us to listen to them. But we trusted them. That was a complete killer. And you feel insecure, like, I need Barneys to be cool. And then there are these things called R.T.V.s.”
  R.T.V. stands for “return to vendor,” which is what it sounds like: If a collection — the one that the store has asked you to pad out with novelty and exclusives — doesn’t sell, the retailer can return it and ask for its money back. According to Nutter, as stores struggled, the terms of this deal got worse. In some cases, stores asked designers to sell on consignment or to share costs if a certain percentage of the collection didn’t sell at full price. So let’s say a store decided to mark the collection down early: You now owed it for those losses. “Even as I’m telling you this,” Nutter said, “I’m like, Isn’t that crazy?”
 It is. It is crazy. And here’s where it got even crazier: In order to protect exclusivity, stores had to commit to even larger buys, ordering more clothes than they could possibly sell. Then, when they couldn’t move the stuff, they’d return it. Thanks to the rise of fast fashion and the luxury market’s simultaneous attempt to keep up with its impossible pace, it all started to feel disposable. So detrimental was the cycle of overproduction and discounting to luxury goods that in 2018, Burberry, the British label, revealed that it had been burning — not metaphorically but literally:  burning — $37 million of worth of merchandise per year to maintain “brand value.”
  In short, fashion seemed to slowly annihilate itself. Remember fashion week? While incurring all those losses, designers were still putting on shows roughly every three months, productions that ran hundreds of thousands of dollars. (Or millions, if you were Chanel.) The problem is that everyone who attended the shows and streamed them out via endless blurry Instagram videos was actively making the case for the demise of their jobs. Because if you’re there watching via the tiny screen on your phone while the real live show is happening feet away, why even go? “God bless fashion media,” Sternberg said. “They still have not caught up to the idea that everyone is seeing it at the same time.”
 “It’s such a little scam, fashion week,” he continued. “I love doing shows, but you get caught up in it. And then you can’t stop. Because if you stop, they’re going to write about you stopping, and you’re going to look like a failure. Or the stores will stop buying your stuff, and you don’t really know why they’re buying your stuff, but they’re buying it. And you’re not relevant anymore if you’re not doing a show.”
 Sternberg acknowledged that there were other factors that killed Band of Outsiders, chief among them his own inexperience in scaling a niche brand, but ultimately he was underfunded and overleveraged. The day he opened the store in SoHo — with a Momofuku Milk Bar attached — he knew it was over. Sternberg took a $2 million convertible loan from CLCC, a fashion fund backed by a Belgian shipping magnate, and defaulted six months later. The brand was collateral. (Band has since been reborn as a zombie version of itself, run by the Belgians.) In May 2015, he handed off passwords, keys and a storage locker in Pomona, Calif., with the brand’s archive and walked away. “But it wasn’t some big disaster,” he said. “Well ... by the end it was a little bit of a disaster.”
 Sternberg’s story was not unique among his peers. In Europe, luxury fashion conglomerates like LVMH and Kering paired young designers with experienced businesspeople. “In America, it was much more entrepreneurial,”  Andrew Rosen, a founder of Theory and an early investor in  Proenza and Rag & Bone, told me. “You had a lot of these incredibly talented young designers that frankly didn’t have the business partnership to go along with it.”
 I asked Sternberg if he felt as if he’d lost the narrative. “To some extent, I didn’t lose the n

哦，从来没有想过这个假设：一起喝醉的行为可能是一种社交技术，通过抑制别人的高级认知功能来帮助我们验证他们的可信度，从而使我们更难有意识地伪造东西。这是有道理的。
&gt；为了增强我们的自然薄切能力，因此人类还发展了各种文化实践，使这些即时评估更加可靠。这些技术利用了这样一个事实，即欺骗本质上是一种冷认知行为，依赖于认知控制中心。这意味着，如果我们能够削弱我们正在努力判断的人的认知控制能力，我们就会更好地查明他们：他们将不太能混淆我们的作弊检测系统。
在一项已被证明对执法机构非常有用的研究中，研究人员发现，如果嫌疑人被要求以相反的顺序提供不在场证明，从最近发生的事件开始，然后往回走，警察可以显著提高他们识别虚假陈述的能力。在这项研究中，研究人员发现，如果嫌疑人被要求以相反的顺序提供不在场证明，那么他们可以显著提高识别虚假陈述的能力。这不是我们通常讲故事的方式，所以被迫讲故事会增加认知负荷。事实证明，如果你以这种方式阻碍他们的意识，不诚实的嫌疑人就是无畏的、有效的说谎者。
这种倒序不在场证明技术是执法的一个很好的工具，但在评估潜在的商业伙伴或决定你即将与之签订和平条约的人是否真诚时，它并不是非常实用。不过，还有其他方法可以达到同样的效果。这项警察研究的目的是通过增加负载量来降低受试者的认知控制能力-可以说是增加了更多的重量。或者，你可以保持负荷量不变，但通过抑制认知控制中心来降低认知控制能力--削弱基本肌肉。要做到这一点，一种方法是经颅磁刺激(TMS)，即在脑壳的适当区域施加强大的磁力。然而，TMS是一项非常新的技术，并没有完全广泛使用。此外，在大多数文化中，用巨大的磁铁震撼新认识的人的头被认为是不礼貌的。
要想产生同样的效果，一种更低技术、更能被社会接受的方法是让某人完全醉倒。正如我们在第六章中所讨论的，酒精和其他麻醉剂的主要作用之一是下调或暂时麻痹与认知控制相关的前额叶皮质区域。一杯龙舌兰酒相当于一杯TMS的液体。因此，各式各样的麻醉剂经常被人类用作社交润滑剂并不是偶然的。酒精、卡瓦、大麻、魔力蘑菇，应有尽有：任何人们能很快拿到手的麻醉剂都会在正式和非正式的社交场合发挥核心作用。在中国古代，任何重大条约的签订都要先把大家聚在一起，举行一次长时间的、浸泡在酒精中的宴会。事实上，这是中国文化的一个特点，四千年来没有改变过一次。任何希望与中国合作伙伴达成交易的现代商人都最好先让自己的肝脏成形。
&gt；在不那么正式的层面上，这无疑是为什么醉酒者是各种人类社交聚会的普遍特征，从休闲的鸡尾酒会到兄弟会的交谊会。喝醉不仅令人愉悦，而且通常还会让人们更自由、更容易地相处(至少在一定程度上，之后酒后打架就会爆发)。醉酒至少在两个方面增强了合作。首先，它通过抑制认知控制中心来减少社交伪装。其次，如果我们都喝醉了，我们就会创造一种相互脆弱的局面，从而使信任更容易建立。醉酒本质上是一种解除精神状态的行为。就像和别人握手保证你没有拿着武器一样，喝下几杯龙舌兰酒就像在门口检查你的前额皮质一样。看见?。没有认知控制。你可以相信我。
note_en:
Ohhh, never thought of this hypothesis: that the act of gettingdrunk together might be a social technology that helps us verifythe trustworthiness of others by inhibiting their higher cognitivefunctions and thus making it harder to consciously fake things.That would make sense.
 > To enhance our natural thin-slicing abilities, humans havetherefore also developed various cultural practices that make theseinstant assessments more reliable. These techniques take advantageof the fact that deception is fundamentally a cold-cognition actand relies on cognitive control centers. This means that if we canimpair the cognitive control abilities of people we're trying tojudge, we’ll do a better job of sussing them out: they willbe less able to confuse our cheater-detection systems.
 > In one study that has proven enormously useful to lawenforcement agencies, researchers found that police officers couldsignificantly improve their ability to detect false statements ifsuspects were asked to give their alibis in reverse order, startingwith the most recent event and working their way back. This is notthe way we normally tell stories, so being forced to do itincreases cognitive load. Dishonest suspects, it turns out, areless effective liars if you handicap their conscious minds in thisway.
 > This reverse-order alibi technique is a great tool for lawenforcement but not terribly practical when evaluating a potentialbusiness partner or deciding if the people you’re about tomake a peace treaty with are being sincere. There are other ways toachieve the same effect, though. The police study aimed to reducesubjects’ cognitive control ability by increasing theload-adding more weight, as it were. Alternately, you can keep theload constant but decrease cognitive control ability-weaken themental muscles-by suppressing cognitive control centers. One way todo this is transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS), which involvesapplying a powerful magnetic force to the appropriate region of theskull. TMS, however, is a very recent technology and not exactlywidely available. Also, in most cultures it’s considered badform to shock the heads of new acquaintances with huge magnets.
 > A much more low-tech and socially acceptable way to producethe same effect is to get someone completely wasted. As wediscussed in chapter 6, one of the primary effects of alcohol andother intoxicants is to "downregulate," or temporarily paralyze,areas of the prefrontal cortex associated with cognitive control. Acouple shots of tequila is the liquid equivalent of a nice jolt ofTMS. It’s therefore no accident that intoxicants of varioussorts are frequently employed by human beings as social lubricants.Alcohol, kava, cannabis, magic mushrooms, you name it: anyintoxicant that people can get their hands on quickly comes to playa central role in social occasions, both formal and informal. Inancient China, no major treaty was signed without first bringingeveryone together in an extended, alcohol-soaked banquet. In fact,this is one feature of Chinese culture that has not changed a bitin over four thousand years. Any modern businessperson hoping toink a deal with Chinese partners had better get his or her liver inshape first.
 > On a less formal level, this is no doubt why intoxicantsare a universal feature of all sorts of human social gatherings,from casual cocktail parties to fraternity mixers. Not only isgetting drunk pleasant, it also typically causes people to getalong more freely and easily (at least to a certain point, afterwhich the drunken fights break out). Intoxication enhancescooperation in at least two ways. First of all, it reduces socialfaking by inhibiting cognitive control centers. Second, if we allget drunk together, we create a situation of mutual vulnerabilitythat makes trust easier to establish. Getting drunk is essentiallyan act of mental disarmament. In the same way that shaking righthands with someone assures them that you’re not holding aweapon, downing a few tequila shots is like checking yourprefrontal cortex at the door. See? No cognitive control. You cantrust me.

研究人员在8月6日的“化学”杂志上报道，通过将带正电的荧光染料配制成一种名为小分子离子隔离晶格(SMILES)的新材料，一种化合物的灿烂发光可以无缝地转变为固态、结晶态。这一进展克服了开发荧光固体的长期障碍，从而产生了现有的最亮的已知材料。印第安纳大学(Indiana University)化学家阿马尔·弗洛德(Amar Flood)与哥本哈根大学的波·劳森(Bo Laursen)共同撰写了这项研究，他说，这些材料在任何需要明亮荧光或需要设计光学特性的技术中都有潜在的应用，包括收获、生物成像和激光。
弗洛德说，除此之外，还有一些有趣的应用，包括上转换光以捕捉太阳能电池中更多的太阳光谱，用于信息存储和光致变色玻璃的可切换光材料，以及可能用于3D显示技术的圆偏振发光。
虽然目前有100,000多种不同的材料可供选择，但几乎没有一种可以以可预测的方式混合和匹配，以创建固体光学材料。染料在进入固态时往往会发生猝灭，这是由于它们紧密堆积在一起时的表现，降低了它们的荧光强度，从而产生更柔和的辉光。
弗洛德说，当染料在固体中肩并肩地站着时，猝灭和染料间耦合的问题就会出现。他们情不自禁地互相摸了摸。就像坐在故事时间里的小孩子一样，他们相互干扰，不再像个人一样行事。
为了克服这个问题，Flood和他的同事们将一种有色染料与无色的氰化星溶液混合，氰化星是一种星形分子，可以防止荧光分子在混合物凝固时相互作用，保持其完好无损。当混合物变成固体时，微笑就形成了，然后研究人员将其生长成晶体，沉淀成干燥的粉末，最后旋转成薄膜或直接结合到聚合物中。由于蓝星大环形成了生成格子状棋盘的积木，研究人员可以简单地将染料插入到格子中，而不需要进一步的调整，结构就会呈现出它的颜色和外观。
虽然之前的研究已经开发出一种使用大环分子来分隔染料的方法，但它依赖于有色的大环来完成这项工作。弗洛德和他的同事们发现，无色的大周期是关键。
弗洛德说，有些人认为无色的大循环不吸引人，但它们允许隔离晶格充分表达染料的明亮荧光，而不受大循环颜色的阻碍。
下一步，研究人员计划探索使用这种新技术形成的荧光材料的特性，使他们能够在未来与染料制造商合作，实现这种材料在各种不同应用中的全部潜力。
弗洛德说，这些材料都是全新的，所以我们不知道它们的哪些固有特性会提供更好的功能。我们也不知道材料的限制。因此，我们将从根本上理解它们是如何工作的，从而为创建新属性提供一套健壮的设计规则。这对于将这些材料交到其他人手中至关重要--我们希望寻求众包，并在这一努力中与其他人合作。更多信息：化学，本森等人：即插即用荧光染料和大环光学材料&34；www.cell.com/chem/fulltext/S2451-9294(20)30310-7，doi：10.1016/j.Chempr.2020.06.029
note_en:
By formulating positively charged fluorescent dyes into a new class of materials called small-molecule ionic isolation lattices (SMILES), a compound's brilliant glow can be seamlessly transferred to a solid, crystalline state, researchers report August 6 in the journal  Chem. The advance overcomes a long-standing barrier to developing fluorescent solids, resulting in the brightest known materials in existence.    "These materials have potential applications in any technology that needs bright fluorescence or calls for designing optical properties, including   harvesting, bioimaging, and lasers," says Amar Flood, a chemist at Indiana University and co-senior author on the study along with Bo Laursen of the University of Copenhagen.
  "Beyond these, there are interesting applications that include upconverting light to capture more of the solar spectrum in solar cells, light-switchable materials used for information storage and photochromic glass, and circularly polarized luminescence that may be used in 3-D display technology," Flood says.
  While there are currently more than 100,000 different   available, almost none of these can be mixed and matched in predictable ways to create solid optical materials. Dyes tend to undergo "quenching" when they enter a solid state due to how they behave when packed close together, decreasing the intensity of their fluorescence to produce a more subdued glow.
  "The problem of quenching and inter-dye coupling emerges when the dyes stand shoulder-to-shoulder inside solids," says Flood. "They cannot help but 'touch' each other. Like young children sitting at story time, they interfere with each other and stop behaving as individuals."
   To overcome this problem, Flood and colleagues mixed a colored dye with a colorless solution of cyanostar, a star-shaped   molecule that prevents the fluorescent molecules from interacting as the mixture solidified, keeping their   intact. As the mixture became a solid, SMILES formed, which the researchers then grew into crystals, precipitated into dry powders, and finally spun into a thin film or incorporated directly into polymers. Since the cyanostar macrocycles form building blocks that generate a lattice-like checkerboard, the researchers could simply plug a dye into the lattice and, without any further adjustments, the structure would take on its color and appearance.
    While previous research had already developed an approach to spacing the dyes apart using macrocycle molecules, it relied on colored macrocycles to do the job. Flood and colleagues found that colorless macrocycles were key.
  "Some people think that colorless macrocycles are unattractive, but they allowed the isolation lattice to fully express the bright fluorescence of the dyes unencumbered by the colors of the macrocycles," says Flood.
   Next, the researchers plan to explore the properties of fluorescent materials formed using this novel technique, enabling them to work with dye makers in the future to realize the materials' full potential in a variety of different applications.
  "These materials are totally new, so we do not know which of their innate properties are actually going to offer superior functionality," says Flood. "We also do not know the materials' limits. So, we will develop a fundamental understanding of how they work, providing a robust set of design rules for making new properties. This is critical for putting these materials into the hands of others—we want to pursue crowd sourcing and to work with others in this effort."        More information:  Chem, Benson et al.: "Plug-and-Play Optical Materials from Fluorescent Dyes and Macrocycles"  www.cell.com/chem/fulltext/S2451-9294(20)30310-7 ,  DOI: 10.1016/j.chempr.2020.06.029

本课程的重点是分析和设计几何折叠的算法。主题包括可折叠结构的重新配置，由铰链连接的一维杆制成的连杆，折叠二维纸张(折纸)，以及展开和折叠三维多面体。本课程还包括建筑、机器人、制造和生物学的应用。

   This course focuses on the algorithms for analyzing and designing geometric foldings. Topics include reconfiguration of foldable structures, linkages made from one-dimensional rods connected by hinges, folding two-dimensional paper (origami), and unfolding and folding three-dimensional polyhedra. Applications to architecture, robotics, manufacturing, and biology are also covered in this course.
